name: Human-in-the-Loop Agent Design
description: Designing agents that know when to pause, request clarification, or escalate to a human rather than proceeding autonomously. The key design problem is calibrating the interruption threshold — too many interruptions kill utility, too few lead to costly unrecoverable errors. Trust calibration, uncertainty quantification, and graceful handoff protocols are the open problems.
contrarian_take: Most HITL papers optimize for minimizing interruptions as if human attention is the only cost — but the real cost is cognitive context switching. A poorly timed interruption that requires a human to reconstruct context from scratch is more expensive than five well-timed ones. Papers that model interruption quality vs. frequency, rather than just frequency, are doing the right work.
example_signals: When Should Agents Ask for Help? Uncertainty-Driven Human Escalation in LLM Agents
Trust Calibration in Human-AI Teaming: A Framework for Agentic Systems
Interrupt or Not? Optimizing Human-Agent Handoff in Long-Horizon Task Completion
